title: Why Some Parts Resist Healing
researcherTopic: meditation and neuroscience, the illusion of the self
metaDescription: Discover why parts of you resist healing after trauma. Learn to understand these protectors not as saboteurs, but as loyal guardians holding the key to wholeness.
imageDescription: A beautifully repaired ceramic bowl, its cracks filled with shimmering gold, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle light illuminates the intricate patterns of repair, symbolizing the beauty of healing and integration.
htmlContent:
The part of you that refuses to heal is not your enemy. It is the only part that remembers the original promise. It holds the memory of a time before the trauma, a moment when the world was not a place of threat but of possibility, and it has been guarding that memory with its life. This is the part that remembers the texture of sunlight on your skin before you learned that visibility was a liability, the part that recalls the effortless expansion of your breath before your chest became a cage.

We spend so much of our energy trying to fix, to manage, to bargain with these parts of ourselves that seem to stand in the way of our own liberation, seeing them as stubborn saboteurs or broken relics of a past we desperately want to escape. But what if this resistance is not a sign of pathology but a significant act of loyalty? What if the parts that resist healing are actually the most devoted protectors of our core essence, refusing to let go of the one thing that the trauma tried to extinguish entirely ~ our wholeness.

In my years of working in this territory, I've seen that the most significant shifts occur when we stop fighting these parts and start listening to what they have been trying to tell us all along.

The Architecture of Internal Resistance

We often imagine the psyche as a unified kingdom, a single self that should, by all rights, want the same thing: peace, connection, a life free from the echoes of the past. Yet, for anyone who has walked the path of healing from complex trauma, the internal field feels more like a fractured territory, a collection of states and parts with competing agendas and deeply held beliefs. What emerges is not a metaphor; it is the lived, felt reality of a system that organized itself around survival. One part of us might be desperate to connect with others, to finally feel a sense of belonging, while another, a younger part, remains convinced that connection is the most dangerous thing in the world because its every attempt at reaching out was met with punishment or neglect. This internal conflict is not a sign of being broken; it is the logical, intelligent outcome of a nervous system that had to adapt to an environment that was fundamentally unsafe and contradictory.

For more on how these internal systems work, you might find our article on understanding internal family systems to be a useful resource.

The Body's Unspoken Veto

As researcher and neuroscientist Sam Harris has explored, the illusion of a single, unified self is one of the most powerful constructs we live with, yet our direct experience, especially in the context of trauma, tells a different story entirely. The part of us that resists healing is often a somatic part, a constellation of sensations, tensions, and impulses that holds the unprocessed energy of the past. It doesn’t speak in words; it speaks in a tight jaw, a shallow breath, a perpetually clenched stomach, a subtle bracing against a world it still perceives as hostile.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link).

The Loyalty of the Protector

Every part of us that seems to be working against our healing is, in fact, a protector. It is a part that took on a difficult and often painful job at a time when we were too young or too overwhelmed to handle the situation ourselves, stepping into a role it has never been able to relinquish. The part that isolates learned that people are dangerous. The part that numbs out with distractions learned that feeling is unbearable.

The part that criticizes and shames learned that if it could just make you perfect, you might finally be safe from external attack. These protectors are not the enemy. They are the heroes of our story, the ones who did whatever it took to ensure our survival, even at great cost to our vitality and connection. The resistance we feel is their loyalty, their unwavering commitment to the job they took on so long ago.

Think about that for a second.

They are like soldiers still fighting a war that ended decades ago, unaware that peace has been declared. To approach them with force, with judgment, with the intention of getting rid of them, is to confirm their deepest fear: that they are bad, that they are the problem, that they must be exiled for us to be whole. A client once described this as trying to fire the one security guard who never fell asleep on the job.

The only way to help these protectors relax their grip is to earn their trust, to show them, through consistent and compassionate action, that we are here now, that we are capable of handling the present, and that they are finally allowed to rest. This process of building internal trust is central to the work we do, as we explore in building internal trust after trauma.

The Illusion of a Singular Self

The Cost of Premature Peace

We are a culture that is deeply uncomfortable with discomfort. We want the fix, the solution, the five-step plan that will take us from pain to peace as quickly as possible. And so, we often try to bypass the resistance, to force a kind of premature peace on our internal systems.

A practical workbook for managing dissociation is Coping with Trauma-Related Dissociation (paid link).

We tell the anxious part to calm down, the angry part to be quiet, the grieving part to get over it. We try to plaster over the cracks with positive thinking or spiritual platitudes, hoping that if we just ignore the resistance, it will eventually go away. But the resistance does not go away. It goes underground.

It morphs into chronic physical symptoms, into depression, into a pervasive sense of emptiness that we cannot quite name. The parts that have been silenced do not disappear; they simply become more entrenched, more convinced than ever that they are on their own, that they cannot trust us to listen. The cost of this premature peace is our own wholeness. We end up living a smaller and smaller life, tiptoeing around the edges of our own experience, terrified of triggering the parts we have exiled.

True healing requires us to do the opposite. It requires us to turn toward the resistance, to become curious about it, to honor the intelligence within it. It requires us to be willing to feel the discomfort of the internal conflict, to hold the tension between the part that wants to move forward and the part that is digging in its heels, and to trust that in that tension, a new possibility can be born. For those struggling with the physical manifestations of trauma, our article on somatic release for trauma may offer some guidance.

The Invitation in the Resistance

Ultimately, the part of you that resists healing is not an obstacle to be overcome. It is an invitation. It is an invitation to slow down, to listen more deeply, to get to know the parts of yourself that you have long since abandoned.

It is an invitation to stop trying to fix yourself and to start building a relationship with yourself, a relationship based on trust, on compassion, on a radical and unwavering acceptance of every single part of your experience. What we find here is not easy work. It requires a kind of courage that is rarely celebrated in our culture, the courage to turn toward our own pain, to sit in the fire of our own contradictions, to stay with ourselves even when every instinct is telling us to run. It is the work of a lifetime, the slow and patient process of reclaiming the lost territories of the self, of gathering the scattered fragments of our own being back into a cohesive whole.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

The resistance is the map. It is showing you exactly where the work is, where the lost child is hiding, where the unprocessed grief is stored, where the deepest healing is waiting to happen. The question is not how to get rid of the resistance.

Wild, right?

The question is whether you are willing to accept the invitation. For those seeking professional support, resources from organizations like the American Psychological Association can be a starting point, and modalities like EMDR have shown significant promise. Further information on trauma and mental health can be found on the National Institute of Mental Health website.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does a part of me seem to sabotage my progress in therapy?

What feels like sabotage is almost always a form of protection. The work is to update its understanding of your current reality, not to fight against its protective instincts.

Can I ever get rid of the parts that resist healing?

The goal is not to get rid of parts, but to heal and integrate them. Through building a trusting internal relationship, these parts can release their burdens and transform from protectors or exiles into valuable, integrated aspects of your whole self.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link).

How can I listen to a part that doesn't speak in words?

Many resistant parts are somatic, meaning they communicate through bodily sensations, postures, and impulses. The key is to approach these sensations with curiosity rather than an agenda to change them.

Is it normal to feel worse when I start to address my trauma?

For those working on vagal tone through breathwork a breathing trainer device (paid link).

What if I can't feel any of my parts?

Numbness or a sense of disconnection is also a protective strategy. The work, in this case, is not to force feeling but to get to know the protector that is creating the numbness, to understand its fears and its reasons for being there.

What will you do now that you know the resistance is not a wall, but a door?
